Client 2: And I want to feel like the monthly cash flow actually works, not just on paper.
Advisor: Perfect. I’ll start high level and then we’ll drill down. On income—what do you two consider your household gross income for the year, all-in?
Client 1: All-in household gross is 149650 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not double-counting—Thomas, your base pay is the bigger chunk and Jennifer you’ve got your W-2 plus some other streams, right?
Client 2: Yeah. My W-2 is 35850, and I also have rental income.

Client 2: Yes, interest and dividends. It’s paid weekly, and annual amount is 23550.
Advisor: Okay. Switching to spending—Jennifer, since you’re the one watching the day-to-day, what do your total monthly expenses usually run?
Client 2: Roughly eight thousand a month—sorry, the exact number we use is 7900 per month.
Advisor: Perfect, I’ll use 7900. Now debt—mortgage, car loans, credit cards—what are the monthly debt payments total?
Client 1: Zero. Monthly debt payments are 0.

Client 2: And I want to feel like the monthly cash flow actually works, not just on paper.
Advisor: Perfect. I’ll start high level and then we’ll drill down. On income—what do you two consider your household gross income for the year, all-in?
Client 1: All-in household gross is 149650 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not double-counting—Thomas, your base pay is the bigger chunk and Jennifer you’ve got your W-2 plus some other streams, right?
Client 2: Yeah. My W-2 is 35850, and I also have rental income.

Client 2: Correct, no mortgage. The house is paid off.
Advisor: Great. Let’s talk assets next—investments and cash. Ballpark, what’s the total investable assets number you think of for the household?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 690300.
Advisor: Okay—now I’m going to break that into the buckets you actually see. Do you have a joint taxable investment account?
Client 1: Yes—joint taxable. The value is 486400, held at a retirement platform.

Client 1: Yes, a private markets position at a bank. Value is 12150.
Advisor: And the home—separate from investable assets—what’s the current value you’re using for your primary residence?
Client 1: 353100. It’s on an advisor platform.
Advisor: Great. Last area: protection. Thomas, do you have any long-term care coverage in place?
Client 1: Yes, long-term care. Monthly premium is 350, coverage amount is 313150, and it’s covered until 2033-09-24.

Client 1: Yes, that’s right.
Client 2: Yep, all zeros on the debt side.
Advisor: Okay—switching gears from debt and the house. Thomas, Jennifer, when markets get choppy, how would you describe your overall risk comfort—conservative, moderate, aggressive, something else?
Client 1: Aggressive. Net-net, we’re fine with volatility if it’s in service of growth.
Client 2: Yeah—aggressive overall, but I still want a decent cash buffer so we’re not stressed month to month.

Advisor: Okay—tell me about the rental. Is that monthly, quarterly…?
Client 2: It comes in weekly, and the annual amount is 27200.
Advisor: Weekly deposits—helpful. Any bonuses we should bake in, even if they’re variable?
Client 1: Yes. There’s a joint annual bonus—53650 a year.
Client 2: And Thomas has his own bonus too—monthly. The annual amount is 45250.

Client 2: I think we’re in the 32%? Or—sorry, I might be mixing that up.
Advisor: No worries. Thomas, what do you have on your side—what bracket band are you planning around?
Client 1: 35%.
Advisor: Got it—thanks. Last household admin item: dependents. Any kids or anyone financially dependent on you right now?
Client 2: No—number of dependents is 0.

Client 2: And I want to feel like the monthly cash flow actually works, not just on paper.
Advisor: Perfect. I’ll start high level and then we’ll drill down. On income—what do you two consider your household gross income for the year, all-in?
Client 1: All-in household gross is 149650 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not double-counting—Thomas, your base pay is the bigger chunk and Jennifer you’ve got your W-2 plus some other streams, right?
Client 2: Yeah. My W-2 is 35850, and I also have rental income.

Advisor: Perfect. So I’ve got: aggressive risk posture, working with an RIA, tax bracket band 35%, and zero dependents. Next time we’ll tie that back to the investment mix and the long-term care coverage you mentioned.
Advisor: Before we wrap today, I want to lock down a couple household basics so the plan is built on the right assumptions. Thomas, Jennifer—what state do you consider your primary residence for planning and taxes?
Client 1: TX.
Advisor: Got it—Texas. And country-wise, you’re in the US, correct?
Client 2: Yes, US.

Advisor: Hi Thomas, hi Jennifer—good to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. Net-net, we want a clear plan—retirement timing, taxes, and just making sure we’re not missing anything big.
Client 2: And I want to feel like the monthly cash flow actually works, not just on paper.
Advisor: Perfect. I’ll start high level and then we’ll drill down. On income—what do you two consider your household gross income for the year, all-in?

Advisor: Got it—Texas. And country-wise, you’re in the US, correct?
Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. And marital status—just confirming, you two are married?
Client 2: Yes, married.
Advisor: Great. And household size—just adults for this question—how many adults are in the household?

Client 1: Right—base. Bonuses are separate.
